title: Contradictions and Consequences of Soviet Reforms in Kazakhstan in the Second Half of the 20th Century and the Policy of Perestroika
overview: Lecture 8 covers the post-war reconstruction, Khrushchev's 1956 'Secret Speech' against Stalin's cult of personality, the extensive (rather than intensive) Soviet economic model, the Virgin Lands campaign (1954) and its demographic and ecological consequences, the catastrophic environmental legacy (Semipalatinsk Nuclear Test Site, Aral Sea desiccation, industrial pollution), the 1979 Tselinograd event and the 1986 December events (Jeltoqsan), and Gorbachev's Perestroika which culminated in the national-democratic movements of 1989–1991.

coreConcepts.definition: Restoration of war-torn economy after 1945: reorientation of mining/metallurgy to peacetime, demobilization, agricultural revival.
coreConcepts.example: Karaganda coal basin (40% of Soviet wartime coal) became the foundation of the new Karaganda Metallurgical Plant; Zhezkazgan Mining and Metallurgical Complex (1947) became one of the country's largest copper producers.

coreConcepts.definition: Turning point in Soviet history: N.S. Khrushchev openly criticized the cult of personality of Stalin and condemned the repressions of his rule.
coreConcepts.example: Mass rehabilitation began; >100,000 people rehabilitated in Kazakhstan.

coreConcepts.definition: Khrushchev's program to plough 'virgin and fallow' lands in northern Kazakhstan to boost grain production.
coreConcepts.example: Over 25 million hectares developed in Kazakhstan; ~2 million people resettled; by 1959 Kazakhs were only 29% of the republic's population.

coreConcepts.definition: Soviet model that increased production by expanding scale rather than improving efficiency or introducing new technologies.
coreConcepts.example: Pavlodar Aluminum Plant (largest in the USSR) – a typical project of extensive growth.

coreConcepts.definition: Main Soviet nuclear weapons proving ground; 456 nuclear tests between 1949 and 1989 (116 atmospheric, 340 underground).
coreConcepts.example: About 1.5 million people suffered effects of nuclear tests, including elevated disease and mortality rates.

coreConcepts.definition: Desiccation of the Aral Sea driven by Soviet cotton irrigation diverting the Syr Darya and Amu Darya rivers.
coreConcepts.example: Continued into the post-Soviet period; >80% loss of surface area by the 2000s.

coreConcepts.definition: Mass protest in Tselinograd against the Soviet leadership's decision to resettle Volga Germans to Kazakhstan as a German autonomous region.
coreConcepts.example: More than 10,000 people took part; the resettlement plan was cancelled. First mass demonstration in Soviet Kazakhstan.

coreConcepts.definition: Protests in Almaty against the appointment of Gennady Kolbin (an outsider) as First Secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan instead of D. Kunaev.
coreConcepts.example: Youth, students and workers demanded reversal of the decision; violently suppressed; Kazakh poet Mukhtar Shakhanov defended the protesters.

coreConcepts.definition: M. Gorbachev's policy of renewal and modernization of the Soviet political and economic system.
coreConcepts.example: In Kazakhstan: democratization, anti-nuclear movement 'Nevada–Semipalatinsk' (1989, Olzhas Suleimenov), 'Azat' movement (1990) for political and economic independence.

coreConcepts.definition: Youth movement of Kazakh students founded in Moscow in 1963 to revive the Kazakh language, culture and national consciousness.
coreConcepts.example: Promoted preservation of national identity among Kazakh students in Russian universities.

expandedNotes.heading: Post-War Years (1945–1953)
expandedNotes.body: After the Great Patriotic War, Kazakhstan faced challenges: restore war-torn economy, demobilize and reintegrate troops, adapt economy to peace. Main task: restore industry – mining/metallurgy especially. The Karaganda coal basin (~40% of Soviet wartime coal) became the foundation for the new Karaganda Metallurgical Plant. Several large state farms and collective farms were established. Zhezkazgan Mining and Metallurgical Complex founded 1947 became one of the country's largest copper producers.

expandedNotes.heading: Khrushchev's Thaw and the 20th Congress (1956)
expandedNotes.body: The 20th Congress of the CPSU in 1956 was a turning point. Khrushchev openly criticized Stalin's cult of personality and condemned the repressions. Mass rehabilitation of victims of political repression began. According to the Committee for the Rehabilitation of Victims of Political Repressions, over 100,000 people were rehabilitated in Kazakhstan – many falsely accused and harshly punished. HOWEVER, the Alash leaders were NOT rehabilitated in the late 1950s. Only during perestroika, from 1988, did the rehabilitation of Alash leaders begin – Bukeikhanov, Baitursynov, and others were officially recognized as victims of Stalinist repressions and their names cleared.

expandedNotes.heading: Zhas Tulpar (1963)
expandedNotes.body: Youth movement of Kazakh students founded in Moscow in 1963 to revive Kazakh language, culture and national consciousness. Active in promoting preservation of national identity among Kazakh students, with support inside Kazakhstan.

expandedNotes.heading: Extensive Soviet Economy and Industry
expandedNotes.body: Post-war Soviet economy continued to develop extensively – production increased by expanding scale rather than improving efficiency. In Kazakhstan this meant intensive development of new lands and creation of large industrial enterprises. Pavlodar Aluminum Plant – largest aluminum producer in the USSR – became the striking example. LIMITATIONS: lack of attention to modernization and new technologies; decreasing competitiveness of Soviet products on the world market; constant shortages and resource deficiencies affecting the standard of living. CONTRIBUTION TO USSR INDUSTRY: Karaganda Metallurgical Plant, Pavlodar Aluminum Plant; the mining industry developed rich deposits of coal, copper, iron ore; Zhezkazgan Mining and Metallurgical Complex.

expandedNotes.heading: Virgin Lands Campaign (Tselina, 1954–1965)
expandedNotes.body: Initiated in 1954 to increase grain production and ensure food security. POSITIVES: over 25 million hectares of virgin and fallow lands developed; grain production rose significantly. NEGATIVES: intensive land use without regard to natural conditions caused soil erosion and decreased fertility; wind and water erosion lost significant areas for agriculture, with productivity falling. SOCIAL EFFECT: mass resettlement caused social and economic difficulties; Kazakhs fell to only 29% of the republic's population by the 1959 census; many settlers faced lack of infrastructure, medical care and schools.

expandedNotes.heading: Environmental Crisis
expandedNotes.body: The environmental situation deteriorated significantly in the post-war years. CAUSES: intensive industrial development; active use of natural resources; absence of effective environmental programs. POLLUTION: Irtysh River – one of Kazakhstan's largest – was significantly polluted by chemical waste from the Pavlodar Chemical Plant, harming the population's health and agriculture. VIRGIN LANDS contributed to soil erosion and fertility loss. NUCLEAR TESTING: the Semipalatinsk Nuclear Test Site conducted 456 nuclear tests between 1949 and 1989 (116 atmospheric, 340 underground); ~1.5 million people suffered effects including elevated disease and mortality. ARAL: drying of the Aral Sea continued through the period.

expandedNotes.heading: Tselinograd Event (1979)
expandedNotes.body: 1979 mass protests in Tselinograd against a Soviet leadership decision to resettle Volga Germans to Kazakhstan as a German autonomous region. The local population saw this as a threat to their social and cultural life. More than 10,000 people took part. The resettlement plan was cancelled. The event demonstrated that the population of Kazakhstan was ready to defend its rights and interests, and foreshadowed broader social-political changes in the republic.

expandedNotes.heading: Jeltoqsan (December Events 1986)
expandedNotes.body: Protests sparked by the appointment of Gennady Kolbin as First Secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan (replacing D. Kunaev). The appointment of an outsider caused discontent among the Kazakh population. Youth, students and workers took to the streets of Almaty demanding reversal of the decision and appointment of a Kazakh national. The protests were violently suppressed; Kayrat Ryskulbekov became the symbol of the resistance and later died in detention. Mukhtar Shakhanov, a Kazakh poet and public figure, spoke in defense of the protesters – his role in exposing the violence and repression and demanding a fair investigation led to the release of many participants and restoration of their rights. The December events foreshadowed broader changes that led to the collapse of the USSR and Kazakhstan's independence.

expandedNotes.heading: Perestroika in Kazakhstan
expandedNotes.body: Initiated by Mikhail Gorbachev in the mid-1980s; in Kazakhstan it brought democratization and economic reform. Increased role of local authorities and greater responsibility for social-economic development. ANTI-NUCLEAR MOVEMENT 'Nevada–Semipalatinsk' – established 1989, led by poet Olzhas Suleimenov – played a key role in ending nuclear testing at Semipalatinsk and drawing international attention to the region's environmental problems. NATIONAL-DEMOCRATIC MOVEMENTS: in 1990 the 'Azat' movement was created, fighting for Kazakhstan's political and economic independence. These movements grew national consciousness and active political participation. 1989: Law 'On Languages' made Kazakh the state language. 25 October 1990: Declaration of State Sovereignty of the Kazakh SSR. 24 April 1990: post of President established; N. Nazarbayev elected. 29 August 1991: Nazarbayev's decree closed the Semipalatinsk Nuclear Test Site. 1 December 1991: first nationwide presidential elections; Nazarbayev victorious. 16 December 1991: Constitutional Law 'On State Independence of the Republic of Kazakhstan'.

keyScholars.name: Nikita Khrushchev
keyScholars.contribution: First Secretary of the CPSU (1953–1964); initiated Virgin Lands campaign and the 'Secret Speech' at the 20th Congress.

keyScholars.name: Dinmukhamed Kunaev
keyScholars.contribution: First Secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan (1960–1962, 1964–1986); member of the Politburo; era of stable development.

keyScholars.name: Gennady Kolbin
keyScholars.contribution: Appointment as 1st Secretary in December 1986 triggered the Jeltoqsan protests.

keyScholars.name: Kayrat Ryskulbekov
keyScholars.contribution: Student participant in Jeltoqsan; convicted, died in detention; posthumously rehabilitated and honored as a People's Hero.

keyScholars.name: Mukhtar Shakhanov
keyScholars.contribution: Kazakh poet and public figure; defender of Jeltoqsan protesters; co-led 'Nevada–Semipalatinsk' movement.

keyScholars.name: Nursultan Nazarbayev
keyScholars.contribution: First Secretary of the Communist Party of Kazakhstan (1989); first President of Kazakhstan from 1990; led the country through independence.

keyScholars.name: Mikhail Gorbachev
keyScholars.contribution: Last leader of the USSR; initiator of perestroika and glasnost.

keyScholars.name: Olzhas Suleimenov
keyScholars.contribution: Poet; founder (February 1989) and leader of the 'Nevada–Semipalatinsk' anti-nuclear movement.

comparativeTables.title: Soviet Era 1953–1991: Major Reforms and Crises
rows: 25 mln ha cultivated; Kazakhs 29% of population by 1959
Revival of Kazakh language/culture among students
First mass national protest of perestroika era

comparativeTables.title: Environmental Disasters of the Soviet Era
rows: Soil exhaustion; loss of arable land

realWorldExamples: 29 August is now commemorated as the International Day Against Nuclear Tests, on the initiative of Kazakhstan (UN resolution 2009).
Independence Monument in Almaty and the 'Tauelsizdik tany' relief commemorate Jeltoqsan.
The 'Nevada–Semipalatinsk' movement led directly to the August 1991 closure of the test site.
Kayrat Ryskulbekov was awarded the title 'People's Hero of Kazakhstan' in 1996.
The Kazakh State Musical Theater and the cinema founded under Cultural Revolution survive as the basis of modern Kazakh stagecraft.
Areas around Semipalatinsk (now Semey) still suffer elevated cancer rates – decades-long radiological consequences.

Nazarbayev becomes 1st Secretary of CC CP Kazakhstan
Law 'On Languages' – Kazakh as state language
Nazarbayev elected President of KazSSR
Declaration of State Sovereignty of KazSSR

Closure of the Semipalatinsk Nuclear Test Site

mustMemorize: 25 million ha cultivated; by 1959 Kazakhs only 29% of population
20th Congress 1956 – Khrushchev criticizes Stalin's cult; mass rehabilitation
1979 Tselinograd event – Volga German autonomy cancelled
Kayrat Ryskulbekov – symbol of Jeltoqsan; People's Hero (1996)
Semipalatinsk Site: 456 nuclear tests 1949–1989; closed 29 August 1991
16 December 1991 – Constitutional Law on State Independence
cheatSheet:
1954 Tselina → 1956 Secret Speech → 1979 Tselinograd → 1986 Jeltoqsan → 1989 Nevada-Semipalatinsk + Language Law → 1990 Sovereignty + Azat → 1991 closure of polygon + Independence
Key leaders: Khrushchev → Kunaev → Kolbin → Nazarbayev (Suleimenov as civic icon)
Ecology: Semipalatinsk + Aral + Irtysh + Virgin Lands erosion
mnemonics: 16-18/12/86 = three December days that shook the steppe
29/8/91 = Polygon closed (29 = N-N-N nuclear no more)
'9-of-9' Suleimenov: 9 founding members of Nevada-Semipalatinsk (mnemonic for poet's role)
